_provenance.note: Phase 017 research was executed as a single-shot `codex exec` delegation; the sk-deep-research reducer never ran. This registry was RECONSTRUCTED by hand from the final deliverables (research/research.md, findings/*.md, recommendation.md). Findings are listed with evidence pointers to the real output files. Confidence scores are estimated retrospectively — they are NOT output from the quality guard pipeline.

resolved_questions_list.question: Current memory system — what does it actually do right now?
resolved_questions_list.resolution: Mapped the 16-stage memory_save pipeline and identified that the system does two jobs at once (narrative journal + retrieval substrate).

resolved_questions_list.question: Value assessment — are the memories themselves useful?
resolved_questions_list.resolution: Some memories are useful for exact resume recovery, but the average narrative memory is not strong enough to justify the current default-save architecture. Saved content is more useful as retrieval hint than canonical document.

resolved_questions_list.question: Redundancy with spec kit documentation — how much overlap?
resolved_questions_list.resolution: High narrative overlap. Unique content is mostly machine metadata, not prose.

resolved_questions_list.question: Retrieval value — does MCP search on memories actually work?
resolved_questions_list.resolution: Live runtime path too brittle; direct SQLite FTS comparison shows memory wins on session-shaped/historical queries, spec docs win on current-packet queries. Retrieval value is real but narrow.
resolved_questions_list.caveat: I'M UNCERTAIN: live production retrieval precision under a healthy embedding runtime may be somewhat better than the local runtime path observed here.

resolved_questions_list.question: Alternative architectures — what are the options?
resolved_questions_list.resolution: Six options evaluated. Ranking: 1st C (wiki-style + thin continuity), 2nd B (minimal memory), 3rd F (full deprecation), 4th D (handover-only), 5th E (findings-only), 6th A (status quo).

resolved_questions_list.question: Migration path — what happens to the 153 existing memories?
resolved_questions_list.resolution: Do not bulk-convert. Freeze old memories as read-only history. Promote only high-value unique material to packet docs. Keep the rest archived and searchable during transition.

resolved_questions_list.question: Integration impact — what breaks if memory is deprecated?
resolved_questions_list.resolution: Primary impact surfaces: /spec_kit:resume, /memory:*, workflow commands (plan/implement/complete/deep-research/deep-review), agent instructions (@context, @speckit, @orchestrate), MCP handlers, schemas, tests, and docs. Migration is doable but not a one-file change.

resolved_questions_list.question: Recommendation — what should we actually do?
resolved_questions_list.resolution: Adopt Option C as target architecture with Option B as retention policy. Canonical narrative → spec docs; memory saves → thin continuity layer. Phased rollout: phase 018 redefines canonical authority, phase 019 replaces default save behavior, phase 020 decides permanence.

findings.title: Memory system has two jobs: narrative journal + retrieval substrate
findings.question: Q1
findings.rationale: The second job (retrieval) is useful; the first job (narrative) is where most redundancy and quality drift lives. Split them by routing narrative into spec docs and keeping the retrieval substrate as a thin layer.

findings.title: Narrative memory sections are mostly redundant with spec kit docs
findings.question: Q3
findings.rationale: Every major memory section (CONTINUE SESSION, PROJECT STATE SNAPSHOT, IMPLEMENTATION GUIDE, OVERVIEW, DETAILED CHANGES, DECISIONS, CANONICAL SOURCES, RECOVERY HINTS) has a clear equivalent in existing spec kit docs. Only MEMORY METADATA is genuinely unique.

findings.title: Memory retrieval wins on session-shaped and historical queries
findings.question: Q4
findings.rationale: 3/10 queries favor memory, 3/10 favor spec docs, 4/10 tie. The retrieval layer has real value that must be preserved even if narrative is deprecated.
findings.caveat: I'M UNCERTAIN: local runtime was degraded; production may perform differently.

findings.title: Option C (wiki-style + thin continuity) is the best architecture
findings.question: Q5
findings.rationale: Best value-to-complexity ratio. Matches operator's real source-of-truth workflow (spec docs already treated as authoritative). Preserves the retrieval substrate that earns its keep.

findings.title: Option A (status quo + fix generator) is not recommended
findings.question: Q5
findings.rationale: Highest complexity for the weakest long-term information architecture. Phase 005 already documents 562 generator defects; fixing them is a sunk-cost bet on the wrong layer.

findings.title: Freeze existing 150 memories rather than bulk-convert
findings.question: Q6
findings.rationale: Bulk conversion has high cost and low value — most content is redundant. Freeze as read-only historical corpus, lazy-promote only high-value unique material (durable decisions, handovers, research findings).

findings.title: Integration impact is wide but tractable
findings.question: Q7
findings.rationale: 12 VERY-HIGH effort files, ~20 HIGH effort, dozens MEDIUM/LOW. Doable but not a one-file change. Largest cost is command/agent contract cleanup, not archive handling.

findings.title: Phased rollout: phase 018 authority → phase 019 runtime → phase 020 permanence
findings.question: Q8
findings.rationale: Three-phase migration: phase 018 redefines canonical authority (narrative → spec docs, old memories read-only), phase 019 replaces default memory_save behavior with thin continuity artifact, phase 020 decides on permanence or full deprecation.

findings.title: Session continuity + exact resume phrasing are the only irreplaceable memory value
findings.question: cross
findings.rationale: The only piece of memory clearly earning its keep is exact continuity retrieval for session-shaped queries. Everything else can move into spec docs. This single finding justifies the 'thin continuity layer' component of Option C.

ruled_out_directions.direction: Bulk-convert all 150 memory files into spec doc anchors
ruled_out_directions.reason: Migration cost exceeds phase budget; most content is redundant

ruled_out_directions.direction: Full deprecation of memory MCP in phase 018
ruled_out_directions.reason: Unsafe while some root packets still rely on memory as the only surviving narrative artifact

ruled_out_directions.direction: Invest in fixing generator-quality bugs from phase 005
ruled_out_directions.reason: Highest complexity for weakest long-term architecture
